edges.target: Enteropathogen DNA Gyrase / Topoisomerase IV (Fluoroquinolone Target)

nodes.description: The bacterial enteropathogens that cause traveler's diarrhea (most often enterotoxigenic E. coli, also Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella) depend on DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV to manage chromosomal topology during replication. Ciprofloxacin traps the enzyme-DNA cleavage complex, generating double-strand breaks that are bactericidal — the basis for empiric fluoroquinolone therapy in moderate-to-severe cases (now constrained by rising resistance).
nodes.id: Enteropathogen DNA Gyrase / Topoisomerase IV (Fluoroquinolone Target)

evidence.snippet: Quinolone antimicrobials are widely used in clinical medicine and are the only current class of agents that directly inhibit bacterial DNA synthesis.

nodes.description: Empiric antibiotic therapy for moderate to severe cases.

evidence.snippet: Empiric antibiotics are rarely warranted, except in sepsis and some cases of travelers' or inflammatory diarrhea. Targeted antibiotic therapy may be appropriate following microbiologic stool assessment.

evidence.reference_title: Recent Antimicrobial Resistance Situation and Mechanisms of Resistance to Key Antimicrobials in Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli.
evidence.snippet: Resistant strains to representative therapeutic agents such as ciprofloxacin and azithromycin have been reported in recent years, and multidrug-resistant ETEC has also emerged.
